intitule: Resource Exercise: Identify and Classify Pronouns

enonce.texte:
Exercise 1: Identifying Pronouns
Read the sentences below. Identify all the pronouns and classify them into: personal, possessive, reflexive, or reciprocal.

questions.texte: She bought herself a new book, and then they gave each other a hug.
We finished our homework, but yours is still on the table.
He looked at himself in the mirror, and then he helped one another with the bags.

intitule: Resource Exercise: Fill in the Blanks with the Correct Pronoun

enonce.texte:
Exercise 2: Complete the sentences
Choose the correct pronoun from the brackets.

questions.texte: John and I are friends. ______ (We/Us) play football every Saturday.
This pen belongs to Mary. It is ______ (her/hers).
The boy hurt ______ (himself/hisself) while climbing the tree.
The two teams played against ______ (each other/ourselves).

enonce.texte:
Real-life problem solving
You are the secretary of a youth group in your neighbourhood in Yaoundé. Two members, Paul and Amina, are arguing. Paul says, "Amina took my phone without asking me." Amina replies, "I found it on the floor. I was going to give it back to him myself." The group leader asks you to write a short report (3-4 sentences) using at least two personal pronouns, one possessive pronoun, one reflexive pronoun, and one reciprocal pronoun.

questions.texte: Write your report. Underline each pronoun you use and label its type (personal, possessive, reflexive, reciprocal).
questions.reponse: Example answer: Paul said that <u>he</u> (personal) was angry because Amina took <u>his</u> (possessive) phone. Amina explained that <u>she</u> (personal) found it and prepared <u>herself</u> (reflexive) to return it. The leader asked <u>them</u> (personal) to trust <u>each other</u> (reciprocal) again.

intitule: Competence Exercise: My Family and Community

enonce.texte:
Writing a personal paragraph
Write a short paragraph (5 sentences) about how you and your family interact with neighbours in your community. Use the following pronouns: we, our, yourselves, each other.

questions.texte: Compose your paragraph. Then, explain why you chose the reflexive pronoun 'yourselves' instead of 'ourselves'.
questions.reponse: Example paragraph: 'We always greet our neighbours in the morning. We help each other during ceremonies. Please take care of yourselves during the rainy season. Our family shares food with them.' Explanation: 'Yourselves' refers to the neighbours (you plural), not to the speaker's family. 'Ourselves' would mean the speaker and family, which is incorrect here.

enonce.texte:
Spot and correct the pronoun mistakes
Each sentence below contains one error in pronoun use. Identify the error and rewrite the sentence correctly.

questions.texte: Myself and John went to the market.
questions.reponse: Error: 'Myself' used as subject. Correction: 'John and I went to the market.'

questions.texte: The girl looked at each other in the mirror.
questions.reponse: Error: 'each other' used for singular subject. Correction: 'The girl looked at herself in the mirror.'
Error: 'my' used without a noun. Correction: 'This bag is mine.'

questions.texte: They blamed theirselves for the accident.
questions.reponse: Error: 'theirselves' does not exist. Correction: 'They blamed themselves for the accident.'

enonce.texte:
Using pronouns in persuasive speech
You are a candidate for class president. Write a short campaign speech (6-8 sentences) to convince your classmates to vote for you. You must use: I, we, you (plural), our, yourselves, each other, themselves.

questions.texte: Write the speech. Then, list all the pronouns you used and justify why each one is appropriate for a campaign.
questions.reponse: Example speech: 'I promise to listen to all of you. We will work together to clean our classroom. You yourselves can propose ideas during meetings. Let us respect each other's opinions. The teachers themselves support this project. Vote for our team!' Justification: 'I' shows personal commitment, 'you' addresses voters directly, 'we' builds unity, 'our' creates belonging, 'yourselves' empowers voters, 'each other' promotes harmony, 'themselves' adds authority.
